pages.full_text:
We had champagne & smoked salmon at Seamark + the Club House gave us a very good meal. Country-dancing followed, + sending off fire lanterns into the sky. John had been spending the last week in a tent, during Maxine's + David's second week after my Headlands let.

	The young danced in a disco in the garage. Jane & Harry (2 crushed vertebrae) slept at the Dolphin while we turned in to the van on Annie's Cottage drive. She had Kate, Jamie, Louisa, Klara & her girls sleeping at the house.
__Sunday July 18th__
Sally joined us at breakfast/coffee in the garden. Before that, I joined Annie in a swim in the sea. After that we drove up to Seamark + said goodbye to John who is off to Sweden tomorrow.
We went over the Dartford Bridge &, having left [?Thorpeness] at 10, managed to get 1.42 [?train] instead of 2.40, eating lunch during the crossing. On past Dunkirk to near Ghent, difficult road
pages.notes: Page continues from a previous spread — opening text has no heading. 'Headlands let' may be a property/holiday rental name. The crossing appears to be a ferry (Channel crossing). 'Thorpeness' is my best reading of the departure location — a village in Suffolk, UK, which fits the context. The train/ferry time '1.42 instead of 2.40' likely refers to a ferry time. Year is uncertain: volume B covers 2003-2019; 'Sunday July 18th' matches e.g. 2004 or 2010 — cannot confirm from this page alone.

pages.full_text:
signs - motorway exit nos. are separately signed. Most annoying poor signposting of campsite - only one tiny sign to Camping Greenpark (Groenpark)**/* at a tiny roundabout. we went back & forth near Dendermonde + passed it at least once. The receptionist said they've not allowed to advertise.
	The loo/washroom is a plastic portacabin on a permanent base with an enormous solar panel - partition 2ft above floor she shelters loo. Cosy & clean but only 2 wide [?locks].
We walked out to the lake for a delicious meal at Mistral, charming shy waitress.
__Saturday__
	Cheek-by-jowl campsite but polite people. Donk = sandy dunes in marshy land. We drive to Temse. The parking machine swallows a euro so we get a free ticket & dash to look at the river - muddy. ¼ mile metal bridge, longest in B. Use of land-peat → rushes for baskets →poplar for matches.
	On then West → St. Amands. Poor signposting. The signpost at a cross roads will point to 10 different restaurants, but not give the names of villages.
pages.notes: Text opens mid-sentence, continuing from left page (about road signs near Ghent/Belgium). 'Groenpark' is the Flemish name in parentheses after 'Greenpark' — this is Camping Groenpark near Dendermonde, Belgium. 'Donk' appears to refer to a place or local geographical feature (donk = sandy dune/elevation in marshy Flemish landscape). 'Temse' is a town on the Scheldt in Belgium. 'St. Amands' is Sint-Amands, also on the Scheldt. '¼ mile metal bridge, longest in B.' — 'B' likely stands for Belgium. The '**/*' after Groenpark appears to be a footnote/annotation marker in the diary. 'Saturday' heading has no date attached; no year determinable from this page alone.
